meta.title: W415 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes Citan W415 engine — K9K diesel (OM607/608) and 1.2-litre petrol. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for the first-generation Citan. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes Citan W415 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W415 engine typically costs £1,500–£3,500 supply only, depending on engine — the petrol M200 sits at £1,500–£2,800, and the diesel OM607/608 K9K at £1,800–£3,500. Fitting adds £650–£910 depending on engine complexity.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes Citan W415 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM607.951 109 CDI (90 bhp) is our top recommendation for a used Citan — it's the most common variant, has the best parts availability, and avoids the additional Euro 6 emissions complexity of the later OM608. For petrol buyers, the M200.711 112 is simple and reliable, though not as common.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes Citan W415?

acceptedAnswer.text: Injector failure and turbocharger wear on the OM607/608 K9K diesel engines, EGR and DPF clogging on vans used predominantly for urban delivery work, and coil pack failure on the petrol M200 engine. The manual transmission is generally robust with regular servicing.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes Citan W415 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the K9K diesel is one of Europe's most common and proven light commercial engines. It powers millions of Renault, Nissan, Mercedes and Vauxhall vehicles across the continent. The key is maintenance: regular oil changes and injector servicing are essential for high-mileage longevity.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes Citan W415 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is an outright 'avoid', but we'd counsel caution on high-mileage pre-2019 OM607 diesels without documented injector or turbo history — injector and turbo repairs can quickly exceed the van's value on budget examples. The OM608 is more modern but adds AdBlue complexity.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W415 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Rough running or loss of power (K9K injector failure), whining noise or loss of boost (K9K turbocharger wear), DPF warning lights (K9K EGR/DPF clogging), or misfires (M200 coil packs) are the key warning signs. Check engine lights on any variant should be diagnosed immediately.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes Citan W415 engine, or should I scrap the van?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends entirely on the van's value and remaining useful life. Against a well-kept £4,000 Citan, a £1,800–£3,200 OM607 recon is 45–80% — borderline. Against a tired £2,500 example, the same recon exceeds value — walk away. The petrol M200 recon at £1,500–£2,800 is cheaper but less common. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line: value the van's remaining commercial life first.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes Citan W415 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM607 K9K diesel is proven to exceed 200,000 miles with diligent maintenance — injector and turbo servicing are the keys to longevity. The OM608 is too new for definitive data but shares the same core design. The M200 petrol can reach 150,000+ miles with proper servicing.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W415 and the W420?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W415 (2012–2021, this page) is the first-generation Citan, a badge-engineered Renault Kangoo built in France. The W420 (2021–present) is the second-generation Citan, a jointly-developed van built in Spain. The W420 offers newer engines (including electric options) and significantly more interior space.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes Citan W415 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate under the bonnet and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible on the OM607/608 K9K engine (located at the front of the engine block). Tell us your variant (e.g., 109 CDI) and approximate registration year (pre- or post-2019) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes Citan W415 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first-generation Citan panel van — from the Renault-sourced OM607 diesel to the 1.2-litre M200 petrol and the later OM608 update. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the K9K diesel that powered thousands of UK delivery fleets. Backed by verified Mercedes diesel enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes Citan W415 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W415 Citan — from the entry-level diesel to the 1.2-litre petrol and the later OM608 update.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: OM607/608: 85% confidence — well-documented Renault K9K diesel with established failure patterns across the Renault-Nissan-Mercedes alliance. M200: 70% confidence — pattern-data tier; lower-volume petrol engine.

overview.intro: The W415 is the first generation of the Mercedes-Benz Citan, introduced in October 2012 as the successor to the Vaneo. Developed as part of the Daimler-Renault-Nissan Alliance, it is a badge-engineered variant of the second-generation Renault Kangoo, assembled alongside it in Maubeuge, France. Offered in panel van, Mixto (glazed van), and Traveliner (MPV) body styles across three lengths, it served as Mercedes' entry-level commercial vehicle. The range was powered predominantly by Renault's K9K 1.5-litre diesel in OM607 and OM608 forms, with a 1.2-litre petrol available in some markets.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Renault Kangoo II (shared with Nissan NV250) • Predecessor: Mercedes-Benz Vaneo (W414) • Successor: Mercedes-Benz Citan W420 (2021–present) • Sibling body styles in this generation: Panel Van, Mixto (glazed van), Traveliner (MPV).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The OM607/608 K9K diesel engine family remains one of the most-requested light commercial engines in our data, with enquiries concentrated on the 109 CDI as these high-mileage vans exit fleet service.

items.quote: The later K9K diesel offers the most power in the range — 116 bhp and 270 Nm of torque — with improved emissions compliance. Turbocharger and injector wear are its known weak points, but the K9K is one of Europe's most common diesel engines.
items.whoItsFor: fleet buyers who need the most capable Citan diesel.

items.quote: The 1.2-litre four-cylinder petrol offers simple, reliable motoring without the DPF and injector concerns of the diesels — ideal for urban delivery work where diesel emissions compliance is a headache.
items.whoItsFor: urban delivery drivers who want petrol simplicity.

items.quote: The early K9K diesel is a high-mileage workhorse, but injector failure and turbocharger wear are common on vans over 100,000 miles — and EGR/DPF clogging is a persistent issue for urban delivery work.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who accept the risks of a high-mileage diesel and have service history to prove maintenance.

items.quote: The most powerful K9K diesel offers the best performance, but the added complexity of the Euro 6 emissions system — including AdBlue — means more components to fail and higher repair costs.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who need maximum diesel power and accept the emissions complexity.

items.quote: The mid-range diesel offers genuine versatility with 90 bhp and good fuel economy — and sits in the same used price bracket as lower-powered examples. It's the sweet spot of the diesel range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the most common, best-supported Citan diesel.

items.quote: Often ignored in favour of the diesels, the 1.2-litre petrol offers simple, reliable motoring without the DPF and injector concerns — and lower purchase prices because buyers overlook it.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol reliability without the diesel's complications.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid Diesel — more power, same risks.
Euro 6 Diesel — cleaner but more complex.
Most Powerful Diesel — best performance, most complexity.

economicsRule.title: The W415 rule
economicsRule.text: A £1,800–£3,500 OM607/608 recon against a £3,000–£8,000 W415 sits at 25–115% of the van's value — on sub-£4,000 examples, it approaches or exceeds our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. The petrol M200 recon at £1,500–£2,800 is the cheapest option. These are commercial vehicles; value the van's remaining useful life first, because the engine can easily be worth more than the van on budget examples.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W415 Costs Least To Own?
Which W415 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 111 CDI (OM608) — Euro 6 diesel complexity and AdBlue costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W415 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 109 CDI (OM607, 90 bhp) — best-supported diesel without the Euro 6 complexity.

problems.title: K9K Diesel Injector Failure
problems.affectedModels: W415 108 CDI, 109 CDI, 111 CDI (all OM607/608 variants).
problems.rootCause: The K9K diesel's injectors are a known weak point — they can fail due to carbon buildup or electrical issues, leading to rough running, loss of power, and in extreme cases, engine damage.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of rough running or loss of power on any K9K-powered Citan, have the injectors inspected — a £400–£1,000 repair is far cheaper than the £2,500+ replacement that follows if injector failure damages the engine.

cta.label: Compare K9K engine prices for your W415 Citan →

problems.title: K9K Turbocharger Wear
problems.affectedModels: W415 108 CDI, 109 CDI, 111 CDI (all OM607/608 variants).
problems.rootCause: The K9K's turbocharger is subject to wear from high-mileage use, particularly in delivery applications where the engine is frequently started from cold and run at idle — oil supply issues can lead to turbo failure.
problems.recommendation: If your K9K-powered Citan develops a whining noise or loss of boost, have the turbo inspected immediately — a £400–£1,000 repair is far cheaper than the £2,500+ replacement that follows if the turbo fails catastrophically.

cta.label: Compare K9K turbo prices for your W415 Citan →

problems.title: K9K EGR/DPF Clogging (Urban Use)
problems.affectedModels: W415 108 CDI, 109 CDI, 111 CDI (all OM607/608 variants, particularly urban delivery vehicles).
problems.rootCause: The K9K's EGR valve and DPF are susceptible to clogging in vehicles used predominantly for urban stop-start driving — this can trigger warning lights, reduced power, and eventually, limp mode.
problems.recommendation: On any Citan used predominantly for urban delivery, have the EGR and DPF inspected regularly — a £300–£800 cleaning is far cheaper than the £2,500+ replacement that follows if clogging causes engine failure.

cta.label: Compare K9K EGR/DPF prices for your W415 Citan →

problems.title: M200 Petrol Coil Pack Failure
problems.rootCause: The M200 1.2-litre petrol's coil packs are a known weak point — failure leads to misfires, rough running, and check engine lights.
problems.recommendation: If your 112 petrol Citan develops a misfire, have the coil packs checked immediately — a £300–£600 repair is far cheaper than the £1,500+ replacement that follows if misfire damages the catalytic converter.

cta.label: Compare M200 engine prices for your W415 Citan →

replacementCosts.h2: W415 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the baseline figures — the W415 shares its Renault Kangoo platform with the OM607/608 K9K diesel, and engine-bay access is consistent across the range.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W415 Citan only (2012–2021). The second-generation W420 (2021–present) is covered on its own Generation Page. Note the engine power output changes between the pre-2019 OM607 and post-2019 OM608 diesel variants: 108 CDI went from 75 bhp to 80 bhp; 109 CDI from 90/95 bhp to 95 bhp; 111 CDI from 110 bhp to 116 bhp. The M200 1.2-litre petrol was not available in all markets.

mostRequestedEngines: 2. OM608.915 (109 CDI, 95 bhp, post-2019) — pattern data

liveFeed.issue: Whining noise from turbo / loss of boost
